sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 4-year-old mother of twins, presents with a history of injuring her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She reports that her knee popped and has been painful ever since, with pain on the outside and a feeling of instability that causes her to worry that it will buckle.
She is unable to bear weight on it, bend it, or straighten it fully.
She has been taking ibuprofen for pain, which provides some relief.
She has a history of being active, chasing her children, and skiing.
She is generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other significant medical conditions.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Examination of the heart reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmur.
Lung exam is clear to auscultation.

Right knee exam reveals edema and an effusion, pain to palpation, decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses, and no leg edema in the ankle.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is likely suffering from a possible anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury to her right knee, which is causing instability and pain.
Problem list: ACL injury; right knee pain; knee instability; right knee effusion; right knee edema.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain management.
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis of an ACL injury.
I advised the patient to use a knee brace to provide support and stability to the knee.
I will review the MRI results and discuss further treatment options, including surgical repair, in the next appointment.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again soon to review the MRI results and discuss further treatment options.
